dialogue.utterance: Dispatching Units 3 and 7 to a multi-vehicle crash at East Broad and Ninth Street involving a critically ill patient.
Hi, I'm EMT Alex. Can you tell me what happened or any changes in the patient's condition?
The man was hit by a vehicle driving on the wrong side of the road and thrown into a storm drain.
Open your eyes, sir. Please look at me if you can.
Can you squeeze my hand once if you understand me? Please try.
Can you tell me where you are or what your name is? Please respond.
Eyes open to verbal command, no tracking. No verbal or motor response. GCS of 6.
Airway is partially obstructed with blood and teeth. Immediate suctioning required.
Initial pulse was 120, now pulseless. Rapid decline in cardiovascular status.
Agonal respirations observed. Rate estimated at 4 per minute, poor tidal volume.
Spo2 unmeasurable. Airway requires immediate management due to severe obstruction.
Breath sounds absent on the left and diminished on the right. Suspect pleural pathology.
Skin is pale, cool with weak carotid pulse. JVD noted. Rapid decompensation expected.
Le Fort IV fracture, deformed mandible. Pupils 6mm, fixed and dilated.
No petechiae or purpura noted on skin. Multiple abrasions seen on the back.
Airway filled with blood and teeth. No noted obstruction beyond oropharynx.
Right-side chest has crepitus. Breath sounds absent on left, diminished on right.
Abdomen is rigid and distended. Tone is severely impacted.
Suspected bilateral humeral fractures. Pelvis is unstable. No signs of femoral fractures.
Male in his 50s. Hit by vehicle on the wrong side of the road and propelled into a storm drain.
Patient was breathing inadequately and unresponsive but had initial motor response.
Rate improved slightly to 10 per minute. Still shallow and labored from trauma.
EKG registered PEA at 20:09. Progressed rapidly towards asystole.
EKG shows PEA-to-asystole rhythm. No electrical activity sustained after initial interventions.
Post suctioning, O2 saturation to 90% with NRB at 15 LPM. No further improvement seen.
Blood pressure is now unmeasurable. Initially 118/56. Central and peripheral pulses absent.
Oropharyngeal airway inserted. Airway suctioning performed for blood and debris.
Needle decompression on right side at fifth intercostal mid-axillary. Blood return confirmed.
Follow-up EKG remains in PEA, progressing toward asystole. No return to pulse with decompression.
Intubation performed using SALAD technique and bougie-assisted direct laryngoscopy.
Epinephrine 1:10,000 administered via IO at 20:11 and 20:14. Doses per protocol.
IO access established in the left humeral head. Drip start at 500mL normal saline.
500mL normal saline administered in an attempt to salvage circulation.
SpO2 remains 94%. No return of effective breathing, still in persistent PEA/asystole.
Rechecking airway and breathing post-decompression and after intubation.
Initiating Protocol 2-7 for cardiac arrest and Protocol 4-1 for general trauma.
Transport destination VCU Health Systems to handle confirmed traumatic PEA and critical injuries.
Patient placed on backboard with padding. Initial spinal precautions maintained.
Spo2 94%, pulseless. EKG shows transitioning rhythm.
Next dose of epinephrine 1:10,000 IO scheduled.
EMS 102 provided prior NRB and initial care. Red patient with PEA confirmed.
Fire and police on scene. Secured for transport under red tagging.
Compressions ongoing. Using single provider CPR method and cycling between providers.
Severe facial, abdominal, and thoracic trauma with PEA. Traumatic arrest confirmed.
No spontaneous breathing or motor response observed with resuscitation.
No return of pulse after full intervention. Still in PEA to asystole.
Head trauma confirmed. Pupils remain fixed, airway unsecured through trauma.
4-lead ECG monitoring initiated. Continues to run per protocol.
No change in SpO2 post-intubation. Still at 94% with high-flow oxygen.
Respiration at 10 via BVM supported. Evidence of respiratory distress post-decompression.
Baseline glucose was unobtainable. Still unrecorded on re-check.
Respiration from 4 to 10 via improvements. No more than clinical suspicion for PEA at onset.
One vehicle had a DOA in the driver seat. No direct impact to our patient.
Multi-vehicle crash, patient was a pedestrian. Struck from the wrong side of the road.
HEENT confirmation per trauma. No noted obstruction after intubation.
Final EKG before transport in asystole. No resumption of rhythm.
Final blood pressure 0/0. No central or peripheral pulse for duration after 20:11.
Additional findings of abdominal rigidity, right-side pleural injury.
CPR ongoing during transport. Provider pair rotations to reduce fatigue maintaining quality.
Transport initiated with all interventions. Took resuscitation measures en route.
Continuing under Protocol 2-7 for cardiac arrest. PEA-to-asystole confirmed.
No restoration of rhythm. EKG continues to note asystole throughout en route.
Patient intubated at 20:15. Epinephrine administered at scheduled 3-minute intervals.
VCU expected transfer. Additional teams will follow up for trauma protocol.
